questions.scenario:
A teammate runs `kill 4123` to stop a long-running data export, but the process keeps running. Frustrated, they run `kill -9 4123` and the process disappears. They conclude *plain `kill` is broken on this server*.

**What actually happened?**

options.text: `kill` defaults to `SIGTERM`, which the export had a handler for and chose to ignore (or it was mid-syscall and didn't service it). `SIGKILL` cannot be caught, so the kernel killed it unconditionally.
`kill` without `-9` only sends a request to the shell, not to the process
The process was a zombie, and only `SIGKILL` reaps zombies
`SIGTERM` requires root; `SIGKILL` does not

questions.explanation: `kill` with no signal sends `SIGTERM` (15), which is *catchable*. Well-behaved daemons trap it to flush, close connections, and exit cleanly, but they can also legitimately ignore it for a while (e.g. mid-transaction). `SIGKILL` (9) and `SIGSTOP` (19) are the only signals the kernel will not deliver to user-space handlers, so they always succeed. The lesson: `kill -9` is not *more powerful*; it is *non-negotiable*. Use it last, not first.

questions.scenario:
Your service writes a large in-memory cache to disk during shutdown so it can warm-start next boot. Operations runs `systemctl stop myapp` and you notice the cache file is missing on restart.

**Which is the most likely cause?**

options.text: The service didn't trap `SIGTERM`, so systemd's default `SIGTERM` killed it before the shutdown handler ran
The service did trap `SIGTERM`, but its flush took longer than `TimeoutStopSec` (90s default), so systemd escalated to `SIGKILL` and the write was aborted mid-way
Either A or B: both manifest as a missing or truncated cache file on restart, and you cannot distinguish them without checking the journal for the `Killing process …` escalation message
`systemctl stop` always sends `SIGKILL` first, so no graceful path is possible

questions.explanation: Both A and B cause the same symptom (no cache on restart) but have different fixes. A means *write a SIGTERM handler*; B means *raise `TimeoutStopSec=` in the unit file or speed up the flush*. The only way to tell which one bit you is the journal entry, systemd logs `Stopping…`, then either a clean `Deactivated successfully` (handler ran fine but didn't write) or `Killing process X (myapp) with signal SIGKILL` (timeout escalation). Always check the journal before guessing the fix.

questions.scenario:
A Python service that intentionally writes `signal.signal(signal.SIGSTOP, custom_handler)` raises an exception on startup:

```
ValueError: signal only works in main thread of the main interpreter
```

You move it to the main thread and now get:

```
OSError: [Errno 22] Invalid argument
```

**Why?**

options.text: Python's `signal` module does not support `SIGSTOP` on Linux; use `SIGTSTP` instead
`SIGSTOP` cannot be caught, blocked, or handled by user code on any Unix, the kernel rejects the registration. Use `SIGTSTP` (Ctrl-Z) if you want a catchable 'pause' signal.
The process needs `CAP_KILL` to register handlers for stop signals
Errno 22 means the signal table is full; restart the process

questions.explanation: `SIGKILL` and `SIGSTOP` are the two signals the POSIX kernel refuses to let user code interpose on. The kernel needs at least one always-works *kill* and one always-works *suspend* so an operator can never be locked out by a misbehaving program. `SIGTSTP` (terminal stop, what Ctrl-Z sends) *is* catchable and is the right signal for a graceful pause/resume hook.

questions.scenario:
An nginx worker is hung. You run:

```
$ ps -o pid,stat,cmd -p 8112
  PID STAT CMD
 8112 D    nginx: worker process
```

You send `kill -9 8112`. The PID does not go away.

**Why doesn't `SIGKILL` work?**

options.text: You need root; SIGKILL respects file permissions

The `D` state means the process is in uninterruptible sleep waiting on the kernel (typically I/O). `SIGKILL` is delivered, but the kernel only acts on it when the process returns from kernel space, fix the underlying I/O (NFS, dying disk) and the kill will take effect.
nginx workers ignore SIGKILL by default; send the signal to the master instead
PID 8112 is actually a thread, not a process, so SIGKILL is queued forever

questions.explanation: `D` state (uninterruptible sleep) is the one situation where even `SIGKILL` appears to do nothing. The kernel has the process blocked in a syscall (commonly NFS, a dying disk, or a misbehaving FUSE mount). Pending signals are checked when control returns to user space, if it never returns, the signal sits there. The actual fix is to unblock the I/O (kill the NFS server, remount, replace the disk). A long-lived `D` process is a system health signal, not a process problem.

questions.scenario:
A worker container has a child Python script that needs to clean up a tempdir on shutdown. The container's `ENTRYPOINT` is:

```
ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/bash", "-c", "python /app/worker.py"]
```

When Kubernetes sends `SIGTERM`, the cleanup never runs.

**What is the root cause?**

options.text: Python ignores SIGTERM by default; you must register a handler

The shell wrapper becomes PID 1 inside the container. PID 1 has special signal semantics: it does not get default handlers, and bash does not forward signals to its child. SIGTERM hits bash, which shrugs, and python is killed by the eventual SIGKILL.
Kubernetes sends SIGTERM to the container, not to the process; the container runtime is the bug
Python on Linux requires `--init` flag to receive signals

questions.explanation: PID 1 is special: the kernel will not deliver any signal that PID 1 has not explicitly installed a handler for, including the *default* terminate behavior. So `SIGTERM` to a bash PID 1 does nothing because bash didn't trap it. Worse, bash does not forward signals to its child either. Fix options: (1) `ENTRYPOINT ["python", "/app/worker.py"]` so python is PID 1 and can register its own handler; (2) `exec python /app/worker.py` inside the shell so python *replaces* bash; (3) use `tini` / `--init` as an init that forwards signals.

questions.scenario:
Your application writes `signal(SIGCHLD, SIG_IGN)` at startup *because* it forks short-lived helpers and you don't want to deal with reaping. Months later, someone tries `wait()` in a debug build and it always returns -1 with `errno=ECHILD`, even immediately after a fork.

**Why?**

options.text: Setting `SIGCHLD` to `SIG_IGN` tells the kernel to auto-reap children, which is exactly what you wanted, but it also means there are *no zombies for `wait()` to harvest*, hence ECHILD. The two behaviors are coupled.
`wait()` is broken on glibc when SIGCHLD is ignored; use `waitpid()` instead
ECHILD means the child finished before the parent could call wait(); use `WNOHANG`
Forking inside a signal handler is undefined behavior and corrupts the process table

questions.explanation: Linux gives `signal(SIGCHLD, SIG_IGN)` a special meaning: it instructs the kernel to auto-reap exited children so they never become zombies. The side effect is exactly what the question describes, `wait()` cannot find a child to harvest because there are none. If you also want `wait()` to work, the right primitive is `sigaction(SIGCHLD, ...)` with `SA_NOCLDWAIT` *or* an empty handler that just returns. `SIG_IGN` with the auto-reap behavior is a single-purpose tool.

questions.scenario:
Your team is reviewing a service's signal handling design. The service: serializes a checkpoint to disk, has a 60s upper bound on that flush, runs as PID 1 in a container, and is started by Kubernetes.

**Which design choices are correct? (Select all that apply.)**

options.text: Trap `SIGTERM` and run the checkpoint flush
Trap `SIGKILL` as a last-resort fast-flush path
Set Kubernetes `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` to at least 60 (and ideally 90)
Make sure the service binary is PID 1 itself, or use an init like `tini`, so the signal actually reaches it
Trap `SIGINT` and treat it the same as `SIGTERM`, so `Ctrl-C` in a `kubectl exec -it` shell behaves predictably

questions.explanation: (a) is the standard graceful-shutdown pattern. (c) gives the trap enough time to finish before Kubernetes escalates to SIGKILL. (d) avoids the PID-1-eats-signals trap covered in the previous question. (e) is good hygiene: many CLIs send SIGINT, and treating both the same makes behavior consistent. (b) is the only wrong answer: `SIGKILL` cannot be trapped on any POSIX system. Any code claiming to handle it is dead code.
